original_abstract:
Tabular data, typically organized in rows and columns, serves as the backbone of empirical research across domains as varied as biomedicine, particle physics, and climate science. A core challenge in these fields is the prediction of missing labels based on available features—a task critical for drug discovery, risk assessment, and materials design. While deep learning has fundamentally reshaped our ability to learn from raw sensory data like images and text, the domain of tabular data has been dominated for over two decades by gradient-boosted decision trees.

In this work, we introduce the Tabular Prior-data Fitted Network (TabPFN), a tabular foundation model that shifts the paradigm of algorithm development. Rather than being manually engineered, TabPFN is a learning algorithm that is itself learned by training a transformer on millions of diverse, synthetic datasets. Our results demonstrate that TabPFN outperforms all existing state-of-the-art methods on datasets with up to 10,000 samples by a substantial margin, while requiring orders of magnitude less time. For instance, in just 2.8 seconds, TabPFN achieves higher classification accuracy than an ensemble of the strongest baseline models tuned for four hours.

Beyond its speed and predictive power, TabPFN functions as a versatile generative foundation model, enabling secondary capabilities such as fine-tuning, data generation, density estimation, and the creation of reusable embeddings. By providing superior modeling performance across heterogeneous domains, TabPFN offers a scalable path toward accelerating scientific discovery and improving automated decision-making in high-stakes environments.
humanized_abstract:
Most empirical research in biomedicine, particle physics, climate science and other fields is based on tabular data, i.e., data displayed in tables with rows and columns. The task of predicting missing output variables from observed input variables is therefore ubiquitous and of prime importance in e.g., drug discovery, risk assessment and materials design. While deep learning has revolutionized learning from large amounts of heterogeneous, mostly visual or textual, data in recent years, for the past two decades the standard method for analyzing tabular data has been gradient-boosted decision trees.

Most machine learning algorithms for tabular data are human-engineered, designed to exploit domain-specific knowledge such as pairwise or higher-order relationships between variables. Training such algorithms from scratch for each problem is often infeasible. Prior-work has therefore focused on adaptable, efficient variants. Here, we introduce the Tabular Prior-data Fitted Network (TabPFN), a new class of foundation models for tabular data, revolutionizing the way we engineer algorithms. In contrast to current practice, our learning algorithm for designing the network architecture itself is learned, by training a transformer on a massive set of synthetic datasets. Results on thirteen standard benchmark datasets with up to 10,000 samples show that our method significantly outperforms the state of the art, while being a orders of magnitude faster. For example, our model trains to an accuracy surpassing the best strong baselines in less than 3 seconds, while having tuned these for 4 hours each.

While TabPFN demonstrates rapid speed and accurate prediction, it can be employed as a multiscale generative foundation model for other uses cases such as the fine-tuning, data synthesis, posterior sampling for density estimation, and representation learning. As it demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on modeling highly diverse sets of phenomena, TabPFN holds great promise in the pursuit of accelerating the process of scientific discoveries and high-stakes decision making under uncertainty.

Tabular data, typically organized in rows and columns, serves as the backbone of empirical research across domains as varied as biomedicine, particle physics, and climate science. A core challenge in these fields is the prediction of missing labels based on available features—a task critical for drug discovery, risk assessment, and materials design. By providing superior modeling performance across heterogeneous domains, TabPFN offers a scalable path toward accelerating scientific discovery and improving automated decision-making in high-stakes environments.
